title: Yield curve likely to steepen as the UK changes prime minister again
gist: Oxford Economics expects the UK gilt yield curve to steepen in coming months as Andy Burnham—the clear favourite to replace Starmer as PM by September—is perceived to favour looser fiscal policy, and pre-election tightening embedded in current fiscal plans is expected to be watered down.

themes.theme: UK Political Transition and Yield Curve Steepening

insights.claim: Keir Starmer's resignation means the UK will have a new Prime Minister by September at the latest, with Andy Burnham as the clear favourite; Oxford Economics does not plan major changes to its July baseline forecast given the unlikely time to develop a detailed policy platform before the autumn budget.
insights.speaker: Oxford Economics
insights.so_what: Oxford sees the Burnham transition as a political process story rather than an immediate macro pivot; investors should not front-run major fiscal loosening for the autumn budget, but should position for the directional shift in the yield curve that market perception alone will drive.
insights.quote: Keir Starmer's resignation means the UK will have a new Prime Minister by September at the latest, with Andy Burnham the clear favourite.

insights.claim: Oxford Economics expects the UK gilt yield curve to steepen in the next few months due to perceptions that Burnham might pursue looser fiscal policy combined with fading short-end pressures, and believes pre-election fiscal tightening in current government plans will be watered down.
insights.speaker: Oxford Economics
insights.so_what: A steeper UK yield curve reflects a fiscal credibility discount for the incoming Burnham government; positioning for UK curve steepening (long front-end, short long-end gilts) is the actionable trade that Oxford Economics implies—a bearish signal for UK long-end bonds.
insights.quote: Given the UK's poor fiscal position, perceptions that Burnham might pursue looser fiscal policy, and fading short-end pressures, we expect the yield curve to steepen in the next few months.
insights.locator: Given the UK's poor fiscal position
